revision.text:
'''三好三人衆'''は日本の戦国時代に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
三好氏の頭領[[三好長慶]]の死後台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺に携わるなどした。
[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず三人衆の勢力は衰えた。

revision.text:
'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の戦国時代に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
三好氏の頭領[[三好長慶]]の死後台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺に携わるなどした。
[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず三人衆の勢力は衰えた。
[[Category:戦国時代|みよしさんにんしゅう]]

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
三好氏の頭領[[三好長慶]]の死後台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺に携わるなどした。
[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず三人衆の勢力は衰えた。
[[Category:三好氏|*]] [[Category:戦国時代 (日本)|みよしさんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数|3みよしさんにんしゆう]]
[[zh:三好三人衆]]

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
三好氏の当主[[三好長慶]]の死後、後継者の[[三好義継]]が幼かったためその後見役として台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺に携わるなどした。のちに義継や久秀と対立し、[[三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となった。
[[1566年]]、[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、三好三人衆は完全に消滅した。なお、三人衆の一人である政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣となり、[[大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死したことが知られている。
[[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将|*みよしさんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数|3みよしさんにんしゆう]]
[[zh:三好三人衆]]

[[1566年]]、[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、三好三人衆は完全に消滅した。なお、三人衆の一人である政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣となり、[[大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死したことが知られている。

三好氏の当主[[三好長慶]]の死後、後継者の[[三好義継]]が幼かったためその後見役として台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺（[[永禄の変]]）に携わるなどした。のちに義継や久秀と対立し、[[三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となった。
[[1566年]]、[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、三好三人衆は完全に消滅した。なお、三人衆の一人である政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣となり、[[大坂の役|大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死したことが知られている。

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
三好氏の当主[[三好長慶]]の死後、後継者の[[三好義継]]が幼かったためその後見役として台頭し、[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺（[[永禄の変]]）に携わるなどした。のちに義継や久秀と対立し、[[三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となった。
[[1566年]]、[[織田信長]]の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、三好三人衆は完全に消滅した。なお、三人衆の一人である政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣となり、[[大坂の役|大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死したことが知られている。
[[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将|*みよしさんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数|3 みよしさんにんしゆう]]
[[ko:미요시 산닌슈]] [[zh:三好三人眾]]

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。三好氏の当主[[三好長慶]]の死後、後継者の[[三好義継]]は幼く、長慶の弟達もまたこの世を去っていたため、義継の後見役としてこの三名が台頭し、三好氏の重臣として同じく権勢を振るっていた[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺（[[永禄の変]]）に携わるなどした。のちに義継や久秀と対立する。これは[[三好政権|三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
[[1566年]]、織田信長の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三好三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。長逸は歴史からそのまま姿を消したが、政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣として復帰し、[[大坂の役|大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死している。
[[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将|*みよしさんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数|3 みよしさんにんしゆう]]
[[ko:미요시 산닌슈]] [[zh:三好三人眾]]

[[1566年]]、織田信長の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三好三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。長逸は歴史からそのまま姿を消したが、政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣として復帰し、[[大坂の役|大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死している。

revision.comment: /* 関連項目 */ 本圀寺の変を追加
revision.text:
'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は日本の[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に畿内で活躍した、[[三好長逸]]・[[三好政康]]・[[岩成友通]]の三人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であったためこの名で呼ばれた。
長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。三好氏の当主[[三好長慶]]の死後、後継者の[[三好義継]]は幼く、長慶の弟達もまたこの世を去っていたため、義継の後見役としてこの三名が台頭し、三好氏の重臣として同じく権勢を振るっていた[[松永久秀]]と共に[[足利義輝]]の謀殺（[[永禄の変]]）に携わるなどした。のちに義継や久秀と対立する。これは[[三好政権|三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
[[1566年]]、織田信長の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の二人も消息不明な状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三好三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。長逸は歴史からそのまま姿を消したが、政康はのちに[[豊臣氏]]の家臣として復帰し、[[大坂の役|大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死している。
== 関連項目 ==
*[[三好政権]] *[[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] *[[信貴山城#信貴山城の戦い|信貴山城の戦い]] *[[本圀寺の変]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:みよしさんにんしゆう}} [[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将|*みよしさんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数3]]
[[ko:미요시 산닌슈]] [[zh:三好三人眾]]

[[三好長慶]]の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は幼く、長慶の弟達もこの世を去っていたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭し、三好氏の重臣として同じく権勢を振るっていた[[松永久秀]]と共に[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]の[[暗殺]]（[[永禄の変]]）に携わるなどした。後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは[[三好政権|三好・松永政権]]の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
[[永禄]]9年（[[1566年]]）、織田信長の[[上洛]]に反発し、抵抗するが敵せず相次いで敗退し、三好三人衆の勢力は衰え、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、他の2人も消息不明な状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三好三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。長逸は歴史からそのまま姿を消したが、政康は後に[[豊臣氏]]の家臣として復帰し、[[大坂の陣]]に参加し戦死している。

== 関連項目 == *[[三好政権]] *[[本圀寺の変]] *[[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] *[[筒井城の戦い]] *[[東大寺大仏殿の戦い]] *[[信貴山城#信貴山城の戦い|信貴山城の戦い]] *[[野田城・福島城の戦い]] *[[信長包囲網]] *[[多聞山城]]

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に[[三好長慶]]の死後に[[三好政権]]を支えて[[畿内]]で活動した[[三好長逸]]・[[三好宗渭]]・[[岩成友通]]の3人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であったためこの名で呼ばれた。 == 概要 == 三好長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭している。彼らは三好氏の重臣として同じく権勢を振るっていた[[松永久秀]]と共に、永禄8年（[[1565年]]）の[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]の[[暗殺]]（[[永禄の変]]）に関わるなどした。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好・松永政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[元亀]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。
== 関連項目 == *[[三好政権]] *[[本圀寺の変]] *[[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] *[[筒井城の戦い]] *[[東大寺大仏殿の戦い]] *[[信貴山城#信貴山城の戦い|信貴山城の戦い]] *[[観音寺城の戦い]] *[[野田城・福島城の戦い]] *[[信長包囲網]] *[[多聞山城]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:みよしさんにんしゆう}} [[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:名数3]] [[Category:戦国武将の名数|3みよし]]

後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好・松永政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に[[三好長慶]]の死後に[[三好政権]]を支えて[[畿内]]で活動した[[三好長逸]]・[[三好宗渭]]<ref>宗渭の実名は一般には「政康」とされるが、史料から確認される実名は「政勝」後に「政生」である。これに伴い、従来宗渭（政康）の弟[[三好為三|為三]]の実名を「政勝」とする通説も否定されることになった（天野忠幸「三好一族の人名比定について」（初出:天野『戦国期三好政権の研究（初版））』（清文堂、2010年）/改訂所収:天野『増補版 戦国期三好政権の研究』（清文堂、2015年） ISBN 978-4-7924-1039-1））。</ref>・[[岩成友通]]の3人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であり、『[[言継卿記]]』や『[[多聞院日記]]』などの同時代の記録でも「三人衆」と表記されている。 == 概要 == {{File clip | Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg | width = 300 | 53 | 56 | 21 | 17 | w = 6632 | h = 5293 | [[永禄]]8年([[1565年]])12月の''下野入道''([[三好宗渭]])、''主税助''([[岩成友通]])、''日向守''([[三好長逸]])による[[花押]]の[[連署]]。[[東寺百合文書]]より。}} 三好長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。一説によれば、三好長逸は三好一族の長老、岩成友通は[[松永久秀]]と並ぶ家臣団の代表、三好宗渭は元々[[細川晴元]]陣営出身で旧[[細川氏]]家臣団や堺衆とのパイプ役として位置づけられていた<ref>天野忠幸「三好一族の人名比定について」（初出:天野『戦国期三好政権の研究（初版））』（清文堂、2010年）/改訂所収:天野『増補版 戦国期三好政権の研究』（清文堂、2015年） ISBN 978-4-7924-1039-1）</ref>
[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭している。
永禄8年（[[1565年]]）には、長慶の死に乗じて将軍親政を復活させようとした[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]を[[暗殺]]した（[[永禄の変]]）。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。

後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となる。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。

'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に[[三好長慶]]の死後に[[三好政権]]を支えて[[畿内]]で活動した[[三好長逸]]・[[三好宗渭]]{{Efn|宗渭の実名は一般には「政康」とされるが、史料から確認される実名は「政勝」後に「政生」である。これに伴い、従来宗渭（政康）の弟[[三好為三|為三]]の実名を「政勝」とする通説も否定されることになった<ref name="天野2015">{{Cite book|和書|author=天野忠幸|chapter=三好一族の人名比定について|title=増補版 戦国期三好政権の研究|year=2015|origyear=2010|publisher=清文堂|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1}}</ref>。}}・[[岩成友通]]の3人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であり、『[[言継卿記]]』や『[[多聞院日記]]』などの同時代の記録でも「三人衆」と表記されている。
== 概要 == {{File clip | Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg | width = 300 | 53 | 56 | 21 | 17 | w = 6632 | h = 5293 | [[永禄]]8年([[1565年]])12月の''下野入道''([[三好宗渭]])、''主税助''([[岩成友通]])、''日向守''([[三好長逸]])による[[花押]]の[[連署]]。[[東寺百合文書]]より。}} 三好長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。一説によれば、三好長逸は三好一族の長老、岩成友通は[[松永久秀]]と並ぶ家臣団の代表、三好宗渭は元々[[細川晴元]]陣営出身で旧[[細川氏]]家臣団や堺衆とのパイプ役として位置づけられていた<ref name="天野2015" />。
[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭した。
永禄8年（[[1565年]]）には、長慶の死に乗じて将軍親政を復活させようとした[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]を襲撃し、殺害した（[[永禄の変]]）。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となった。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。
== 関連項目 == * [[三好政権]] * [[本圀寺の変]] * [[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] * [[筒井城の戦い]] * [[東大寺大仏殿の戦い]] * [[信貴山城#信貴山城の戦い|信貴山城の戦い]] * [[観音寺城の戦い]] * [[野田城・福島城の戦い]] * [[信長包囲網]] * [[多聞山城]] * [[畿内・近国の戦国時代]]

[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭した。
永禄8年（[[1565年]]）には、長慶の死に乗じて将軍親政を復活させようとした[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]を襲撃し、殺害した（[[永禄の変]]）。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となった。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。

revision.text: [[category:new pages]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] [[Category:Sr]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]] [[ko:미요시 삼인방]] [[zh:三好三人眾]] [[ja:三好三人衆]]

revision.comment: translation from Japanese
revision.text:
[[category:new pages]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.text:
[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."

From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}

Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.

Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.comment: /* 関連項目 */ lk: [[野田・福島の戦い (1570年)]]
revision.text:
'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に[[三好長慶]]の死後に[[三好政権]]を支えて[[畿内]]で活動した[[三好長逸]]・[[三好宗渭]]{{Efn|宗渭の実名は一般には「政康」とされるが、史料から確認される実名は「政勝」後に「政生」である。これに伴い、宗渭（政康）の弟[[三好為三|為三]]の実名を「政勝」とする従来の通説も否定されることになった<ref name="天野2015">{{Cite book|和書|author=天野忠幸|chapter=三好一族の人名比定について|title=増補版 戦国期三好政権の研究|year=2015|origyear=2010|publisher=清文堂|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1}}</ref>。}}・[[岩成友通]]の3人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であり、『[[言継卿記]]』や『[[多聞院日記]]』などの同時代の記録でも「三人衆」と表記されている。
== 概要 == {{File clip | Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg | width = 300 | 53 | 56 | 21 | 17 | w = 6632 | h = 5293 | [[永禄]]8年([[1565年]])12月の''下野入道''（[[三好宗渭]]）、''主税助''（[[岩成友通]]）、''日向守''（[[三好長逸]]）による[[花押]]の[[連署]]。[[東寺百合文書]]より。}} 三好長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。一説によれば、三好長逸は三好一族の長老、岩成友通は[[松永久秀]]と並ぶ家臣団の代表、三好宗渭は元々[[細川晴元]]陣営出身で旧[[細川氏]]家臣団や堺衆とのパイプ役として位置づけられていた<ref name="天野2015" />。
[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭した。
永禄8年（[[1565年]]）には、長慶の死に乗じて将軍親政を復活させようとした[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]を襲撃し、殺害した（[[永禄の変]]）。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となった。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三好政権]] * [[本圀寺の変]] * [[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] * [[筒井城の戦い]] * [[東大寺大仏殿の戦い]] * [[信貴山城#松永久秀の時代|信貴山城の戦い]] * [[観音寺城の戦い]] * [[野田・福島の戦い (1570年)]] * [[信長包囲網]] * [[多聞山城]] * [[畿内・近国の戦国時代]]
{{デフォルトソート:みよしさんにんしゆう}} [[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将の名数3|みよし]] [[Category:三好政権]]

== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

=== References === {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.comment: Removing this excess
revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]
[[Category:qq-Miyoshi Trio-qq]] [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] qq (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] qq (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] qq (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pgoodbye $1]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] qq (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

=== References === {{Reflist}}

== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
