[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}

Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

[[Category:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyoshi Trio]][[Category:11]] <!--Miyoshi Trio--> [[Category:Zh]] [[Category:Ko]] '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=draft}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=draft}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=draft}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=draft}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."

From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}

Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--Miyoshi Trio-->
'''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=draft}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=draft}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[shōgun]], [[Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]] <!--Miyoshi Trio-->

59 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:三好三人衆 up to rev 103644228
revision.text:
'''三好三人衆'''（みよしさんにんしゅう）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に[[三好長慶]]の死後に[[三好政権]]を支えて[[畿内]]で活動した[[三好長逸]]・[[三好宗渭]]{{Efn|宗渭の実名は一般には「政康」とされるが、史料から確認される実名は「政勝」後に「政生」である。これに伴い、宗渭（政康）の弟[[三好為三|為三]]の実名を「政勝」とする従来の通説も否定されることになった<ref name="天野2015">{{Cite book|和書|author=天野忠幸|chapter=三好一族の人名比定について|title=増補版 戦国期三好政権の研究|year=2015|origyear=2010|publisher=清文堂|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1}}</ref>。}}・[[岩成友通]]の3人を指す。いずれも[[三好氏]]の一族・重臣であり、『[[言継卿記]]』や『[[多聞院日記]]』などの同時代の記録でも「三人衆」と表記されている。
== 概要 == {{File clip | Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg | width = 300 | 53 | 56 | 21 | 17 | w = 6632 | h = 5293 | [[永禄]]8年([[1565年]])12月の''下野入道''（[[三好宗渭]]）、''主税助''（[[岩成友通]]）、''日向守''（[[三好長逸]]）による[[花押]]の[[連署]]。[[東寺百合文書]]より。}} 三好長慶の時代は、それぞれが軍を率い一族の重鎮として活動していた。一説によれば、三好長逸は三好一族の長老、岩成友通は[[松永久秀]]と並ぶ家臣団の代表、三好宗渭は元々[[細川晴元]]陣営出身で旧[[細川氏]]家臣団や堺衆とのパイプ役として位置づけられていた<ref name="天野2015" />。
[[永禄]]7年（[[1564年]]）の三好長慶の死後、甥で後継者の[[三好義継]]は年若すぎ、長慶の弟達も死去していたため、義継の後見役としてこの3名が台頭した。
永禄8年（[[1565年]]）には、長慶の死に乗じて将軍親政を復活させようとした[[室町幕府]]13代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義輝]]を襲撃し、殺害した（[[永禄の変]]）。
後に[[篠原長房]]・[[三好康長]]や[[筒井順慶]]と組んで義継や久秀と対立、これは三好政権の混乱の一因となり、[[足利義昭]]を擁立する尾張の[[織田信長]]を利する結果となった。
永禄11年（[[1568年]]）、信長の[[上洛]]に反発し抵抗するも相次いで敗退し、三人衆の勢力は衰える。永禄末には宗渭が病没、[[天正]]年間には友通が戦死し、程なく長逸も消息不明の状態となり、畿内における三好氏の勢力の衰退と前後して三人衆としての活動は完全に途絶えた。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三好政権]] * [[本圀寺の変]] * [[滝山城 (摂津国)#滝山城の戦い|滝山城の戦い]] * [[筒井城の戦い]] * [[東大寺大仏殿の戦い]] * [[信貴山城#松永久秀の時代|信貴山城の戦い]] * [[観音寺城の戦い]] * [[野田・福島の戦い (1570年)]] * [[信長包囲網]] * [[多聞山城]] * [[畿内・近国の戦国時代]]
{{デフォルトソート:みよしさんにんしゆう}} [[Category:三好氏|*さんにんしゆう]] [[Category:戦国武将の名数3|みよし]] [[Category:三好政権]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8192653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|qq=|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|qq=|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]
{{translated page|ja|三好三人衆|version=103644228|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Miyoshi clan]]
}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8192653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]
{{translated page|ja|三好三人衆|version=103644228|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
[[Category:Miyoshi clan]]
}}

Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."

From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}

They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]
{{translated page|ja|三好三人衆|version=103644228|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8192653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the [[:en:Kinai|Kinai]] region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]]. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the [[:en:Miyoshi clan|Miyoshi clan]] and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of [[:en:Miyoshi Nagayoshi|Miyoshi Nagayoshi]], each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside [[:en:Matsunaga Hisahide|Matsunaga Hisahide]], and Sōi, originally from the camp of [[:en:Hosokawa Harumoto|Hosokawa Harumoto]], served as a liaison to the old [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]] and the merchant city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]].{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), his nephew and successor [[:en:Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu]] was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]], [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiteru|Ashikaga Yoshiteru]], in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and [[:en:Tsutsui Junkei|Tsutsui Junkei]], which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]], who supported [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshiaki|Ashikaga Yoshiaki]]’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. == Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]]
'''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan}} who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai}} region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan}} and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]]
'''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan}} who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai}} region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan}} and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
== Notes == === Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Miyoshi Regime]] * [[Honkokuji Incident]] * [[Battle of Takiyama Castle (Settsu Province)|Battle of Takiyama Castle]] * [[Battle of Tsutsui Castle]] * [[Battle of the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji]] * [[Siege of Shigisan Castle]] * [[Battle of Kannonji Castle]] * [[Siege of Noda and Fukushima Castles]] * [[Anti-Nobunaga Alliance]] * [[Tamonyama Castle]] * [[Sengoku Period in Kinai and Surrounding Provinces]]

All three were members of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan}} and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi|1=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto|1=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan|1=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|1=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru|1=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei|1=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki|1=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter. With the decline of the Miyoshi clan’s influence in the Kinai region, the activities of the three lords came to an end.
