<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سه ژنرال میوشی]][[ja:三好三人衆]][[ko:미요시 삼인방]][[sr:Мијоши тријумвират]][[zh:三好三人眾]]
'''Miyoshi Three Lords''' (三好三人衆, ''Miyoshi Sanninshū'') were the three retainers of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan}} who supported the [[Miyoshi regime]] and were active in the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} region during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] following the death of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi|1=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}. These individuals were [[Miyoshi Nagayasu]], [[Miyoshi Sōi]]{{Efn|While Sōi's given name is commonly thought to be "Masayasu," historical records confirm his name as "Masakatsu," later changed to "Masanari." This revision also overturns the conventional view that Sōi's younger brother [[Miyoshi Tamizou|Tamizou]] was named "Masakatsu." See {{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}.}} and [[Iwanari Tomomichi]]. All three were members of the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan}} and held prominent positions as loyal retainers. Contemporary records, including the ''{{ill|Tsunetsugu Kyōki|ja|言継卿記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunetsugu Kyōki|1=Tokitsugu-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630984}}'' and the ''{{ill|Tamonyō Nikki|ja|多聞院日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonyō Nikki|1=Tamon-in Nikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856341}},'' refer to them collectively as the "Three Lords."
== Overview == {{File clip|Prohibition notice with a joint signature by Miyoshi Nagayasu etc.jpg|width=300|53|56|21|17|w=6632|h=5293|''Kaō'' signatures by ''Shimotsuke Nyūdō'' (Miyoshi Sōi), ''Chikugo no Suke'' (Iwanari Tomomichi), and ''Hyūga no Kami'' (Miyoshi Nagayasu) in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 8 (1565). From the [[Tōji Hyakuyū Monjo]].}} During the era of {{ill|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|en|Miyoshi Nagayoshi|WD=Q1064909|lt=Miyoshi Nagayoshi|1=Miyoshi Nagayoshi}}, each of the three led their own military forces and were key figures within the Miyoshi clan. According to one theory, Nagayasu acted as the elder statesman of the Miyoshi family, Iwanari Tomomichi was a leader of the retainers alongside {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|WD=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, and Sōi, originally from the camp of {{ill|Hosokawa Harumoto|en|Hosokawa Harumoto|WD=Q859769|lt=Hosokawa Harumoto|1=Hosokawa Harumoto}}, served as a liaison to the old {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan|1=Hosokawa clan}} and the merchant city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}.{{Cite book|author=Tadayuki Amano|chapter=Revised Names of the Miyoshi Clan|title=Expanded Study of the Miyoshi Regime During the Sengoku Period|year=2015|publisher=Seibundō|isbn=978-4-7924-1039-1|ref=RefTadayuki2015}}
Following Nagayoshi's death in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), his nephew and successor {{ill|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|en|Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|WD=Q986767|lt=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu|1=Miyoshi Yoshitsugu}} was still a minor, and Nagayoshi’s brothers had also passed away. Consequently, the three lords rose to prominence as Yoshitsugu's guardians.
In {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 8 (1565), they seized the opportunity created by Nagayoshi's death to restore direct rule under the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]]. They attacked and assassinated the 13th {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|en|Ashikaga Yoshiteru|WD=Q447066|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiteru|1=Ashikaga Yoshiteru}}, in what is known as the [[Eiroku Incident]].
Later, they clashed with figures such as [[Shinohara Nagafusa]], [[Miyoshi Yasunaga]], and {{ill|Tsutsui Junkei|en|Tsutsui Junkei|WD=Q8192220|lt=Tsutsui Junkei|1=Tsutsui Junkei}}, which led to internal divisions within the Miyoshi regime. This infighting weakened the regime and ultimately paved the way for {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|WD=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki|1=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}’s rise, to advance into Kyoto.
By {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} 11 (1568), the three lords resisted Nobunaga’s advance but were repeatedly defeated, resulting in the decline of their power. Sōi died of illness in the late Eiroku period, Tomomichi fell in battle during the [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] era, and Nagayasu disappeared soon thereafter.
